coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvent assisted synthesis of N,Cl co-doped carbon dots using longan shell waste: Applications in antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and Fe2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Meng
Wang, Dandan
Liu, Qiuyi
Wang, Luchun
Tao, Yongqing
Wang, Junji
Zou, Yuemeng
Yang, Yulian
Zhou, Quan
Li, Lingling
Wang, Mingyue
Li, Xiang
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Herein, by using longan shell as a precursor, deep eutectic solvent (DES) which consist of ChCl and urea (molar ration, 1:2) as the solvent and dopant, new CDs (Named as N,Cl-CDs) with good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities were synthesized. The chemical studies showed that N,Cl-CDs contained various functional groups (e.g. COOH, OH) and doped with N and Cl. The results of free radical scavenging activity, total antioxidant activity and KMnO4 reduction activity tests showed that N,Cl-CDs had antioxidant properties. The EC50 values were separately determined as 317.26, 302.55 and 20.82 μg/mL, respectively. Because of the good antioxidant activity, N,Cl-CDs also showed redox sensing advantage for oxidants including MnO4 −, Fe3+, Cr6+ and H2O2. Moreover, the classic mouse ear edema inflammation model was further employed to explore the anti-inflammatory activity of N,Cl-CDs. Compared with the normal saline (NS) gup (67.80 %), N,Cl-CDs at high dose (1.40 mg/kg) significantly reduced the percentages of inflammation (38.13 %). Besides, due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching, N,Cl-CDs also showed good sensitivity and selectivity for Fe2+, the limit of detection (LOD) of Fe2+ was 0.3 μM. Finally, the detection of the Fe2+ in water sample was done to test the practical application value of N,Cl-CDs, and the as-developed method showed satisfactory recoveries (92.92 %–111.03 %).

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvent assisted synthesis of N,Cl co-doped carbon dots using longan shell waste: Applications in antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and Fe2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan College of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Mianyang, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan College of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sichuan College of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Mianyang, Sichuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, by using longan shell as a precursor, deep eutectic solvent (DES) which consist of ChCl and urea (molar ration, 1:2) as the solvent and dopant, new CDs (Named as N,Cl-CDs) with good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities were synthesized. The chemical studies showed that N,Cl-CDs contained various functional groups (e.g. COOH, OH) and doped with N and Cl. The results of free radical scavenging activity, total antioxidant activity and KMnO4 reduction activity tests showed that N,Cl-CDs had antioxidant properties. The EC50 values were separately determined as 317.26, 302.55 and 20.82 μg/mL, respectively. Because of the good antioxidant activity, N,Cl-CDs also showed redox sensing advantage for oxidants including MnO4 −, Fe3+, Cr6+ and H2O2. Moreover, the classic mouse ear edema inflammation model was further employed to explore the anti-inflammatory activity of N,Cl-CDs. Compared with the normal saline (NS) gup (67.80 %), N,Cl-CDs at high dose (1.40 mg/kg) significantly reduced the percentages of inflammation (38.13 %). Besides, due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching, N,Cl-CDs also showed good sensitivity and selectivity for Fe2+, the limit of detection (LOD) of Fe2+ was 0.3 μM. Finally, the detection of the Fe2+ in water sample was done to test the practical application value of N,Cl-CDs, and the as-developed method showed satisfactory recoveries (92.92 %–111.03 %).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of society, the global production of biomass waste is gradually increasing, and the management of it has become a major challenge for the whole world [1,2]. Biomass waste is a rich and renewable material that is produced from a variety of sources such as food waste, agricultural and industrial activities [3,4]. In particular, biomass waste from food waste has a huge output, and it often takes a lot of manpower and material resources to deal with [5]. However, in order to save costs, many unscrupulous merchants often dump these wastes directly into the environment, resulting in causing water, soil and other pollution and damage [6]. These can lead to numbers of health problems including food poisoning, respiratory problem and genetic mutations, and even drinking water crises [2,7]. Therefore, it is necessary to take measures to effectively dispose of these biomass wastes and turn them into value-added products.

In the modern world of nanotechnology, the conversion of biomass waste into new nanomaterials for various applications is a promising concept for waste-to-wealth [8,9]. It is considered to be the most efficient and environmentally friendly way to dispose of these wastes, not only to reduce their harm to the environment, but also to functionalize them for other applications such as detection, adsorption and supercapacitor [10–13]. Among many nanomaterials, biomass carbon dots (BCDs) which prepared by using biomass waste as a precursor have gained attention because biomass waste contains a variety of ingredients such as biopolyamines, which is conducive to doping heteroatoms, making it a potential precursor for the preparation of BCDs [14]. Similar to the common CDs [15,16], BCDs also show the exceptional properties of photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability and low toxicity [17–19].

In recent years, researchers have already prepared various BCDs by using different biomass wastes such as palm kernel shell [20] orange peel, magnolia flower, ginkgo biloba and paulownia leaves [21], sugarcane bagasse and taro peel [8] as precursors. These BCDs show good optical properties and have high application value. For example, Wang and co-workers have successfully used some common and cheap biomass waste (e.g. orange peel, ginkgo biloba leaves) to prepare BCDs by using a facile hydrothermal process. These BCDs can be employed to the sensitively detection of Fe3+ [21]. However, it is clear that most of these studies have taken advantage of the optical properties of BCDs, and few studies have explored other properties of these BCDs. In order to make better use of these BCDs, it is necessary to explore their other properties such as pharmacological activities (e.g. antimicrobial, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities) [14,22] to fill the other application gap.

It is reported that BCDs which originate from biomass wastes such as Citrus reticulata peel [23], Pomegranate peel [24] and Carica papaya leaves [25] with various pharmacological activities also show similar pharmacological activities because the residual functional groups of some ingredients in these wastes present on these as-prepared BCDs. In our previous study, by using Impatiens balsamina L. stems which has antibacterial activity as a precursor, a deep eutectic solvent (DES) as solvent and dopant, N,Cl-doped CDs (N,Cl-CDs) was prepared successfully. N,Cl-CDs also showed good antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria [26]. However, researches on the exploration the pharmacological activities of BCDs are still relatively lacking, and the search for BCDs with pharmacological activities is needed to realize the potential of biomass wastes in biomedicine.

Longan (Dimocarpus longan Lour.) is a seasonal tropical fruit, which is also widely used as a Chinese medicine for the treatment of inflammation, diabetes etc. [27]. The nutritious meat of longan is usually widely utilized, while the longan shells are discarded as a by-product, resulting in increasing the ecological burden of society [28]. In this case, longan shells need to be utilized and developed effectively. Longan shells have already been prepared into CDs in previous study [29]. However, similar to the most developed BCDs, researcher only took the advantage of the optical properties of longan shells based CDs. Interestingly, Longan shells have also been reported to have certain pharmacological activities, such as antioxidant [30] and anti-inflammatory [31]. Therefore, inspired by the above developed BCDs with pharmacologically activities, BCDs with similar pharmacological activities with longan shells may also be used by using longan shells as precursors. On the other hand, various studies have already shown that the pharmacological activities and fluorescence properties of the BCDs may be further improved by doping of heteroatoms such as N and Cl [32,33]. In our previous study, we have already found that DES can not only be employed as a green solvent instead of water for the synthesis of CDs, but also doping N, Cl and other elements into the structure of CDs simultaneously [26,34]. Thus, we can try to use different DESs as solvents and dopants on the basis of longan shells as precursors to prepare BCDs with good pharmacological activities especially good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities.

Inspired by the above-mentioned facts, new N,Cl-CDs were successfully synthesized by using DES (ChCl:urea, molar ratio of 1:2) as solvent and dopant, longan shells as precursors. The characterization results demonstrated that the negative charged N,Cl-CDs showed good fluorescence property, as well as rich functional group properties (e.g. COOH, Cl, NH2). The antioxidant abilities of N,Cl-CDs were systematically explored by using DPPH free radical, total antioxidant activity and KMnO4 reduction experiments. Due to the antioxidant effect of N,Cl-CDs, the potential of the N,Cl-CDs for the redox-sensing of a variety of oxidants was also evaluated. Further, the anti-inflammatory activity of N,Cl-CDs was preliminarily assessed by using the ear oedema induced by single application of dimethylbenzene (DMB) animal model. Besides, N,Cl-CDs also showed good sensing performance for Fe2+ through the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of internal filter effect (IFE) and static quenching, and can be adopted for the sensitively detection of Fe2+ in water sample. This is the first study for the synthesis of N,Cl-CDs with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities by using biomass wastes as precursors with DES as a solvent and a dopant.

Longan (Longan shell was separated by us) was purchased from local supermarket (Luzhou, Sichuan). Choline chloride (Purity > 98 %), urea (≥99 %), hexafluoroisopropanol (>99.5 %), glycerin (>99 %), glycol (>99 %) and formic acid (>97 %) were gained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, >97 %) was obtained from Beijing Coolaber Technology Co., Ltd. Ammonium molybdate (>98 %), ascorbic acid (>99 %) and dexamethasone (DXM, >98 %) was manufactured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium monophosphate (>96 %) was gained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Other related oxidant and metal ions were purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. MCF and HepG2 cells were obtained from the Cell Center of the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences. 3- (4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, >98 %) was gained from Beijing Dingguo biotechnology co. Ltd.

Morphology feature of N,Cl-CDs was acquired through a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Functional group and element analysis were conducted on FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1S, Shimadzu) and ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Crystallographic property of N,Cl-CDs was measured through an Empyrean X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Panalytical B.V.). Raman spectrum was gained through a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon S.A.S.). The Zeta-potential was determined by dynamic laser scattering (DLS, Malvern Zetasize). Contact angle experiment was measured through a Contact angle measurement instrument (FCA 2000A AFES, Shanghai Aifei Precision Instrument Co., Ltd). The optical and fluorescence properties were conducted through a UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu) and a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer), respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes were carried out on a lifetime and steady state spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data was gained on Malvern Panalytical Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

DESs were prepared according to the previous reported method [35], which can be seen in Supporting Information. N,Cl-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of the powder of longan shells. In details, 0.5 g of powder was mixed with DES (10 mL). After ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon equipped stainless-steel autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 240 °C for 10 h. After reaction, large particulate products were removed by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 15 min and the filtrate was obtained by filtration through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 7 days and the obtained dialysate was concentrated. Finally, black solid sample (N,Cl-CDs) was gained through drying in a vacuum oven for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation the free radical scavenging activity of N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The study of free radical scavenging activity was conducted according to the classical DPPH assay method with little modification [25]. Specifically, 40 μL of various concentrations (0–500 μg/mL) of N,Cl-CDs (ascorbic acid) was separately added into of 0.1 mM methanolic DPPH (3.95 mL) in the dark. After incubation for 60 min, the UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm was measured and the ascorbic acid was used as a standard. The inhibition percentage and free radical scavenging capacity was acquired through the equation shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation the total antioxidant activity of N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0 mL of the phosphomolybdate reagent (28 mM sodium monophosphate, 0.6 M sulfuric acid and 4 mM ammonium molybdate were mixed in 1000 mL of desalted water) was separately mixed with different concentration (0–500 μg/mL) of N,Cl-CDs or ascorbic acid. The sample was then incubated for 90 min at 90 °C and the absorbance of the cooled sample was recorded at 695 nm. As a comparison, 3.0 mL of deionized water was used as a control. The total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of different concentration of N,Cl-CDs (or ascorbic acid) was calculated from the formula which shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation the KMnO4 reduction activity of N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The antioxidant activity of N,Cl-CDs was determined by using the KMnO4 reduction assay. In details, different concentration (0–500 μg/mL) of N,Cl-CDs (or ascorbic acid) was added into the acidified KMnO4 solution, respectively. After incubating for 30 min in the dark, the absorbance of the mixture was determined at 518 nm. After reduction, KMnO4 solution would change from purple to colorless. The RSA was calculated following the formula below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of oxidants by using N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the sensing abilities of N,Cl-CDs for various oxidants including MnO4 −, Fe3+, Cr6+ and H2O2, 300 μL of each oxidant with different concentration was separately added into N,Cl-CDs solutions (2.7 mL). The fluorescence intensities of solutions before and after adding oxidants were measured. All the fluorescence spectra were gained by using 340 nm as the excitation wavelength.

Mouse model of ear edema induced by DMB was used to explore the anti-inflammatory effect of N,Cl-CDs. Experiments with ear edema in mice were performed according to previously reported methods [36]. Specific steps are as follows: Mice were divided into five groups (n = 5). Control group [normal saline (NS)], positive control group (DXM, 5 mg/kg), high-, medium-, and low-doses of N,Cl-CDs groups (N,Cl-CDs, 1.40, 0.70, and 0.35 mg/kg, respectively). After oral administration once daily for 7 days, 20 μL of DMB (10 μL of the front and rear surfaces) was applied to the right ear locally after 1 h of last oral administration. The mice were killed after 20 min, the round sections with a diameter of 7 mm were gained from the left and right ears with a perforator and weighed. The difference between the weights of the ears indicated the degree of swelling. The percentage of inflammation was determined through the formula below:
P
%
=
MOR - M O L
MOL
× 100
MOR and MOL represented the mass (g) of the circular cross section from the right and left ear, respectively.

MTT measurement was carried out to explore the cytotoxicity of N,Cl-CDs by using MCF and HepG2 cells. Specific steps are as follows: cells were firstly seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Thereafter, different concentrations (100–1000 μg/mL) of N,Cl-CDs were added into the wells and incubated for another 24 h. Subsequently, MTT (10 μL, 5 mg/mL) was separately added into each well and another 4 h incubation was done. Next, the solutions in wells were removed and followed by adding 150 μL of DMSO into each well to dissolve the formazan complex. Finally, after shaking the plates for 10 min, the absorbance was detected at 490 nm by use of a microplate reader (Bio-Tek).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe2+ by using N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the sensing sensitivity of N,Cl-CDs for Fe2+, different concentration of Fe2+ (300 μL) was separately added into the N,Cl-CDs solution (2.7 mL), the fluorescence intensities of solution before and after adding Fe2+ were measured. Besides, different metal ions such as Er3+, Na+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Eu3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Ag+ were selected to explore the sensing selectivity of N,Cl-CDs for Fe2+. The detection steps were same with the steps for the evaluation of sensing sensitivity.
The preparation process and applications of N,Cl-CDs were shown in Scheme 1 .

Studies have shown that doping has influence on the quantum yield (QY), antioxidant or oxidation properties of the CDs [32,26]. These properties would affect the detection effect of the as-prepared CDs on target substances or pharmacological activities of CDs. Therefore, by using longan shells as precursors, CDs with high QY, good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities by introducing DES as the reaction solvent and dopant were intended to be prepared. Herein, several common DESs which contained different elements were used to obtain different doping CDs including Cl,F-CDs, Cl-CDs, N,Cl-CDs and CDs. Table S1 revealed that N,Cl-CDs which synthesized in DES-5 (ChCl/urea, molar ratio of 1:2) showed highest QY (15.2 %, Fig. S1) under the reaction time and temperature of 10 h and 240 °C, respectively. However, the QYs of other CDs were not so satisfied. It also needs to be noted that the QY of CDs (water as solvent) was much lower (3.9 %) than that of N,Cl-CDs (15.2 %), revealing that doping did improve the fluorescence properties of CDs. Thus, DES-5 was selected to prepare N,Cl-CDs under 240 °C for 10 h. Furthermore, the differences in pharmacological activities of N,Cl-CDs and CDs would be compared so as to verify the advantages of N,Cl-CDs.

The morphology of the N,Cl-CDs was evaluated by using TEM. Fig. 1 A revealed that N,Cl-CDs had relatively uniform spherical structure, displaying lattice fringe with lattice spacing of 0.233 nm, which was in line with the graphite (100) plane. This results demonstrated that N,Cl-CDs had a typical graphite-like structure [37]. The crystal size was calculated by using DLS technique, which measures fluctuations of scattered light in colloidal solutions. The DLS spectrum depicted in Fig. S2 showed that the average crystallite size of N,Cl-CDs was 4.55 nm, and the PDI was 0.179. These results demonstrated that the size distribution of N,Cl-CDs was uniform. Further, in order to explore the functional groups, chemical composition s of N,Cl-CDs, FT-IR and XPS analysis were conducted. Fig. 1B showed that absorption bands around 3429 cm−1 and 3260 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH [38]. The peaks located at 2960/2831 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the CH bond [39]. The peak located at 1610 cm−1 was assigned to CO/CN stretching vibration [40]. The absorption bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1101 cm−1 were corresponded to CH bending vibration and CO/CN stretching vibrations, respectively [41,42]. Besides, the absorption band at 775 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-Cl [43].These results revealed N,Cl-CDs were rich in a variety of groups such as NH, OH, CCl, etc. XPS measurement had been further used to explore the chemical bond compositions of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 1C showed that N,Cl-CDs were composed of C 1s (72.41 %), N 1s (14.75 %), O 1s (12.18 %) and Cl 2p (0.66 %), which were separately located at 284.8 eV, 399.64 eV, 531.83 eV and 197.32 eV. The high-resolution C 1s peak showed four peaks locating at 284.5, 285.2, 286.2 and 287.9 eV, which were corresponded to CC, CN, CO/CCl and CO, respectively (Fig. S3A) [44]. The high-resolution N 1s peak can be divided into three peaks locating at 399.1 eV, 399.7 eV and 400.5 eV, which were attributed to the pyridine N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. S3B). The high-resolution O 1s peak can also be split into three peaks locating at 531.3 eV (CO), 532.2 eV (OH) and 533.5 eV (CO) (Fig. S3C) [45,46]. In addition, the high-resolution Cl 2p peak (Fig. S3D) can be deconvoluted into two peaks locating at 196.9 eV (CCl), 198.5 eV (NCl) [43]. It is worth noting that in comparison with the XPS analysis results (Fig. S4) of CDs (water as solvent), the peak of the Cl can hardly see in the XPS spectrum of CDs (water as solvent) and the content of N was significantly improved (from 3.23 % to 14.75 %). These all results revealed that Cl and N were successfully doped in the structure of N,Cl-CDs, and the N,Cl-CDs were rich in a variety of groups such as NH2, COOH, OH, CCl.

Furthermore, XRD pattern measurement was carried out to explore the crystal structure of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 1D revealed that obvious diffraction peaks locating at 25.1°and 42.9° existed in the structure of N,Cl-CDs, which matched with the (002) and (100) lattice spacing [47]. The Raman spectrum of N,Cl-CDs in Fig. 1E further showed that the majority of N,Cl-CDs synthesized were mainly graphitic in nature. The G peak (1544 cm−1) and D peak (1350 cm−1) which separately corresponded to sp2-hybridized carbon and sp3-hybridized carbon existed [48]. Besides, in order to explore the charge characteristic, hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of N,Cl-CDs, zeta potential and water contact angle experiments were conducted. Fig. S5 showed that N,Cl-CDs presented different charging properties under different pH conditions. When pH < 4.56, N,Cl-CDs were positively charged. When pH > 4.56, N,Cl-CDs were negatively charged. These situations may be due to the difference in the quantities of COOH, OH and NH2 on the surface of N,Cl-CDs. The result of water contact angle experiment further showed that N,Cl-CDs had certain hydrophilic property as the contact angle was determined as 54.37° (Fig. 1F), which may also be related to the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of N, Cl-CDs.

UV − Vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence measurement were done to study the optical and fluorescence properties of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 2 A showed that N, Cl-CDs had an absorption peak at 284 nm which corresponded to the n-π* transition of CN/CO. Fig. 2B showed the fluorescence emission spectra of N,Cl-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (from 280 nm to 450 nm). It is obviously seen that the fluorescence spectra of N,Cl-CDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent property, which may be result from the fact that the surface state of N,Cl-CDs would affect the band gap [49]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively (Fig. 2C). Besides, the N,Cl-CDs solution was colorless and transparent in natural light, and emitted bright blue fluorescence in UV light (insert pictures of Fig. 2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging activity of N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH is a stable free radical as nitrogen atoms and lone pair electrons surrounded by three benzene rings exist in its structure. Thus, it has been widely used as a cheap and fast free radical to detect the antioxidant capacity of compound. DPPH appears dark purple, while when it reacts with antioxidant, it will accept the H to form DPPH-H complex, resulting in changing its color to a pale yellow. Therefore, DPPH method can be used to analyze the antioxidant capacity of N,Cl-CDs. In addition, ascorbic acid is a commercial antioxidant and can be used to compare the antioxidant efficiency of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 3 A revealed the absorption spectra of N,Cl-CDs-DPPH mixtures under different concentrations (0–500 μg/mL). With the increase of the concentration of N,Cl-CDs, the characteristic absorption peak at 517 nm gradually decreased. When the concentration of N,Cl-CDs reached 400 μg/mL, the absorption peak at 517 nm almost disappeared. Moreover, with the concentration of N,Cl-CDs increased, the color of DPPH gradually changed from purple to yellow (Fig. 3B). These phenomena indicated that the free radical scavenging ability of N,Cl-CDs increased gradually with the increase of concentration. Fig. 3C showed the free radical scavenging efficiencies of N,Cl-CDs and ascorbic acid at different concentrations. It is clearly seen that the free radical scavenging efficiency of N,Cl-CDs gradually increased from 43.06 % to 93.77 % with the increase of the concentration (100–500 μg/mL). When the concentration of N,Cl-CDs was higher than 400 μg/mL, the free radical scavenging efficiency was higher than that of ascorbic acid. The EC50 value of N,Cl-CDs was calculated as 317.26 μg/mL (Fig. 3D). These results suggested that N,Cl-CDs can be employed as a potential antioxidant. Besides, in order to explore whether doping will affect the free radical scavenging activity of CDs, the free radical scavenging activity of CDs (water as solvent) was also conducted. Fig. S6A showed that with the increase of the concentration of CDs, the characteristic absorption peak at 517 nm gradually decreased. However, only when the concentration of N,Cl-CDs reached 5 mg/mL, the absorption peak at 517 nm almost disappeared. Moreover, Fig. S6B revealed that the free radical scavenging efficiency of CDs increased from 13.10 % to 69.75 % with the increase of the concentration (0.5–5 mg/mL). These all results demonstrated the free radical scavenging activity of CDs was much lower than that of N,Cl-CDs, revealing that heteroatom doping did improve the free radical scavenging activity of the as-prepared CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The total antioxidant capacity of N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphomolybdate assay is a quantitative method to evaluate the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of antioxidant [25]. In order to evaluate the TAC of N,Cl-CDs, different concentration of N,Cl-CDs was separately added into phosphomolybdate reagent, and ascorbic acid was also used as the reference. Fig. 4 A showed that characteristic increases in the absorption at the wavelength of 695 nm occurred in N,Cl-CDs-phosphomolybdate reagent mixture due to the formation of phosphomolybdate V. With the concentration of N,Cl-CDs increased from 0 to 500 μg/mL, TAC increased gradually. Ascorbic acid can achieve higher antioxidant effect at a lower concentration (100 μg/mL), while N,Cl-CDs can achieve similar antioxidant effect to ascorbic acid (100 μg/mL) at a concentration of 500 μg/mL (Fig. 4B). The EC50 values of N,Cl-CDs was calculated as 302.55 μg/mL (Fig. 4C). In addition, Fig. 4D revealed that N,Cl-CDs can reduce molybdenum (VI) to V, thus changing the color of the mixture solution, which may be attributed to the presence of electron-donating groups on the surface of N,Cl-CDs. These all results indicated that although the antioxidant effect of N,Cl-CDs was not as good as ascorbic acid, it still had a certain antioxidant effect. More importantly, as the synthesis of N,Cl-CDs was green and cheap, they can be synthesized in large quantities so that they can be used as cheap antioxidant. In addition, Fig. S7A–S7B further showed that the TAC of CDs (water as solvent) was very low, further indicating that heteroatom doping improved the antioxidant ability of the as-prepared CDs.

KMnO4 reduction assay was further carried out. Fig. 5 A showed that with the increase of the concentration of N,Cl-CDs, significant absorbance decrease can be obtained at 518 nm. The lowest absorbance was gained under the highest concentration of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 5B showed that the RSA (%) of N,Cl-CDs can achieve 96.77 % at the concentration was 100 μg/mL, which can be equal to that of the ascorbic acid. The EC50 values of N,Cl-CDs was calculated as 20.82 μg/mL (Fig. 5C). Moreover, Fig. 5D further showed that N,Cl-CDs may reduce MnO4 − to Mn2+, thus changing the color of the solution. These results indicated that N,Cl-CDs does have certain reducing capacity and can be used as an antioxidant. Similar with the results of free radical scavenging activity, total antioxidant activity experiments, the KMnO4 reduction activity of CDs (water as solvent) was also much lower than that of N,Cl-CDs (Fig. S8).
These all results demonstrated that Cl and N doping can improve the antioxidant ability of N,Cl-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing abilities of N,Cl-CDs for oxidants
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the good antioxidant capacity of N,Cl-CDs, it can be speculated that they may be able to realize the sensing of various oxidants through redox-reaction coupled with other fluorescence quenching mechanism. Therefore, common oxidants including MnO4 −, Fe3+, Cr6+ and H2O2 were employed to investigated the sensing abilities of N,Cl-CDs. Fig. 6 A–6D demonstrated that with the increase of the concentration of oxidants, the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl-CDs aqueous solution gradually weakened, indicating that the fluorescence of N,Cl-CDs can be quenched by using these oxidants. Fig. 6E–6H showed that the fluorescence quenching effects of these oxidants on N,Cl-CDs had good linear relationships in certain concentration ranges. Specifically, linear relationships can be gained ranged from 0 to 80 μM and 90 to 200 μM for MnO4 −. The linear curves were F0/F = 0.0077x + 1.0111(R2 = 0.9219) and F0/F = 0.0949x − 6.5242 (R2 = 0.9888), respectively. There were linear relationships in the concentration ranges of 0–10 μM and 20–400 μM for Fe3+. The linear curves were separately determined as F0/F = 0.0022x + 1.0371 (R2 = 0.9972) and F0/F = 0.0025x + 1.3024 (R2 = 0.9970). The linear relationships can be obtained ranged from 0 to 200 μM and 200 to 600 μM for Cr6+. The linear curves were F0/F = 0.0086x + 1.0171(R2 = 0.9965) and F0/F = 0.0339x − 4.0996 (R2 = 0.9778), respectively. For H2O2, there was a linear relationship in the concentration ranges of 0–30 %. The linear curve was determined as F0/F = 0.0081x + 1.0359 (R2 = 0.9903). Where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities without and with oxidant, respectively. The x was the concentration of oxidant. These results indicated that N,Cl-CDs can indeed realize the detection of oxidants.

It is obviously seen from Fig. 5D that when MnO4 − interacted with N,Cl-CDs, there was indeed a redox process. MnO4 − was reduced to Mn2+, which gradually lightened the color of the solution. Therefore, it can be speculated that N,Cl-CDs can be used for the detection of the selected oxidants by redox-sensing. To deeper understanding whether other fluorescence quenching mechanism exist in N,Cl-CDs-oxidant system, the fluorescence lifetimes, UV–Vis analysis and Stern-Volmer equation were conducted for N,Cl-CDs - KMnO4 system (as a representative). As far as we all know, internal filtration effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET), static quenching and dynamic quenching are regarded as the main quenching mechanisms. When FRET, PET, and dynamic quenching happen, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs with or without quencher will change [50,51]. Thus, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Cl-CDs with and without adding MnO4 − were explored at first. Fig. S9A showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Cl-CDs with and without KMnO4 were determined as 1.81 ns and 1.67 ns, respectively. It is clearly that there was almost no difference in the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Cl-CDs with and without KMnO4, revealing that the FRET, PET and dynamic quenching may not happen, but possibly IFE or static quenching. To explore IFE, UV–Vis was done. Fig. S9B showed that the fluorescence spectra of N,Cl-CDs overlapped with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of KMnO4, revealing that IFE happened [52]. Moreover, Stern-Volmer equation was also carried out to explore the static quenching (Formulas were shown in Supporting Information). Fig. S9C and Table S2 revealed that with the increase of the temperature, the Ksv decreased. In temperature range of 20–40 °C, the Kq was calculated as 1.56 × 1013 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1–1.32 × 1013 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 in 20–40 °C, and the numerical values were much larger than the maximum Kq of collision quenching (1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1). These phenomena revealed that static quenching also existed. In general, the fluorescence quenching of oxidants may occurred through redox-sensing coupled with other fluorescence quenching mechanisms. In the future, the other fluorescence quenching mechanisms for other oxidants can be further explored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N,Cl-CDs on mouse ear oedema
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the good antioxidant property of N,Cl-CDs, a preliminary analysis of the anti-inflammatory property of N,Cl-CDs was explored.The ear edema model was established by ear swelling after DMB exposure. The anti-inflammatory effects of N,Cl-CDs treated groups were compared with those of NS group after the treatment of the DMB for 20 min. Fig. 7 A showed that compared with the percentage inflammation of NS group (67.80 %), different doses (1.40 mg/kg, 0.70 mg/kg and 0.35 mg/kg) of N,Cl-CDs significantly reduced the percentages of inflammation (38.13 %, 46.12 % and 60.11 %). Especially, the percent inflammation results between the high dose (1.40 mg/kg) treatment group of N,Cl-CDs and the DXM (40.32 %) treatment group were close, and the difference between them was not statistically significant. The anti-inflammatory effects of NS, DXM and N,Cl-CDs (high dose) groups can also be seen from Fig. 7B. These results indicated that the N,Cl-CDs had a good anti-inflammatory effect. The anti-inflammatory effects of N,Cl-CDs can be further explored in detail in the subsequent studies, which can further lay a foundation for the subsequent development of anti-inflammatory drugs based on N,Cl-CDs.

To explore the cell toxicity of N,Cl-CDs, MTT measurement was done on MCF and HepG2 cells. Fig. S10 showed that the cell viability of both MCF and HepG2 cells remained over 90 % with the concentration of N,Cl-CDs reaching 1000 μg/mL, revealing that N,Cl-CDs had low cell toxicity. Therefore, N,Cl-CDs may have good potential for the application in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe2+ by using N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the research process, it can be found that N,Cl-CDs can not only realize the sensing of various oxidants, but also Fe2+ also had a quenching effect on the fluorescence of N,Cl-CDs. Therefore, in order to expand the application fields of N,Cl-CDs, the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe2+ on N,Cl-CDs was also preliminarily explored. Firstly, in order to explore the selectivity of N,Cl-CDs on Fe2+, the fluorescence quenching effects of other metal ions were explored. Fig. S11A,B showed that after separately adding different metal ion (1 mM), only Fe2+ showed significantly quenching effects on the fluorescence of N,Cl-CDs, while other metal ions had little quenching effects. These results revealed that N,Cl-CDs showed selectivity towards Fe2+. Secondly, the interaction time between N,Cl-CDs and Fe2+ was optimized so that the N,Cl-CDs can be effectively used for the sensing of Fe2+. Fig. S11C revealed that the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe2+ for N,Cl-CDs can be stable rapidly. Fig. S11D further showed that with the increase of the concentration of Fe2+, the fluorescence quenching effects on N,Cl-CDs gradually increased. It is obviously that a good linear relationship can be obtained in the concentration range of 1 μM–1 mM for Fe2+ (Fig. S11E). The LOD was determined as 0.3 μM referring to the formula of 3σ/ slope (σ represents the standard deviation of the blank solution and slope represents the slope of the correction curve). The linear curve was determined as F0/F = 0.0086x + 1.0648 (R2 = 0.9908). As far as we know, N,Cl-CDs based fluorescence method was not only the first report for the sensitive detection of various oxidants, but also the method showed satisfactory sensitivity for Fe2+. These results indicated that N,Cl-CDs has great application prospect in sensing field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for N,Cl-CDs by Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above in the part of sensing abilities of N,Cl-CDs for oxidants, if the FRET, PET, and dynamic quenching happen, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Cl-CDs before and after adding Fe2+ will change. Fig. S12A revealed that the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Cl-CDs before and after adding Fe2+ only decreased from 2.20 ns to 1.86 ns. Such small change indicated that FRET, PET, and dynamic quenching may not occur during quenching. In order to explore the existence of IFE, the UV–Vis analysis was done. Fig. S12B showed that the UV–vis absorption band of Fe2+ overlapped with the excitation and emission wavelengths of N,Cl-CDs, revealing the existence of IFE. Besides, Stern-Volmer equation was further employed to explore the existence of static quenching mechanism (Equation listed in Supporting Information). Fig. S12C and Table S3 showed that the Ksv decreased as the increase of the temperature, and the calculated Kq (1.13 × 1012–2.15 × 1012 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1) was larger than Kq (1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1), the maximum of collision quenching. The results revealed that the static quenching also existed.

Zeta potential of N,Cl-CDs before and after adding Fe2+ were determined. Fig. S12D showed that after adding Fe2+, the Zeta potential of N,Cl-CDs solution changed from −14.52 to −3.75 mV. This result may be caused by the follow reason: Fe2+ can combine with the COOH and OH groups on the surface of N,Cl-CDs through electrostatic interaction, resulting in forming a non-luminous coordination compound. In this process, the negative charge on the surface of N,Cl-CDs was consumed. Besides, Fe2+ can also interact with amino through complexation. Under these combined actions, Fe2+ can quench the fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the good sensitivity and selectivity of N,Cl-CDs to Fe2+, N,Cl-CDs based fluorescence method was also applied to the sensing of Fe2+ in actual sample. Due to the redox effect of N,Cl-CDs on oxidants such as Fe3+, it can be reduced to Fe2+ in the presence of N,Cl-CDs. Therefore, when Fe3+ exists in the system, other antioxidants such as ascorbic acid can be used first to consume Fe3+, and the concentration of Fe3+ can be calculated by the consumption. When N,Cl-CDs is used for Fe2+ detection, the concentration of Fe3+ converted into Fe2+ can be deducted to obtain the true concentration of Fe2+ in actual sample. Herein, tap water (tap water from our laboratory) was used as the actual sample to explore the practical application potential of N,Cl-CDs. As no Fe2+ and Fe3+ were found in tap water, standard addition method was employed. Adding different concentrations of Fe2+ (60, 80 and 100 μM) into the actual sample, and the relevant results were shown in Table S4. It is clearly that the recoveries of Fe2+ ranged from 92.92 % to 111.03 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 2.61 %, demonstrating that N,Cl-CDs was effective to detect Fe2+.

In summary, N,Cl-CDs were successfully synthesized by using Longan shells as precursors, DES (ChCl:urea, molar ratio of 1:2) as a solvent and a dopant, respectively. The introduction of DES doped Cl in the structure of N,Cl-CDs and significantly improved the contents of N. Due to the doping, the QY of N,Cl-CDs was increased from 3.9 % (water as solvent) to 15.2 %. These heteroatoms doping in the structure also improved the antioxidant properties of N,Cl-CDs in comparison with CDs (water as solvent). Attributed to the good antioxidant activity, N,Cl-CDs were successfully used for the redox sensing of MnO4 −, Fe3+, Cr6+ and H2O2, and wide linear ranges were obtained. The anti-inflammatory results on mouse ear oedema revealed that N,Cl-CDs at high dose (1.40 mg/kg) showed as good anti-inflammatory effect, which was even superior to DXM treatment group, revealing the good anti-inflammatory effect of N,Cl-CDs. Besides, N,Cl-CDs also showed good sensitivity for Fe2+ in linearity ranges of 1 μM-1 mM with a LOD of 0.3 μM. The actual samples analysis results also demonstrated that N,Cl-CDs based fluorescence method showed satisfactory recoveries for Fe2+ detection, revealing the good practical application potential of N,Cl-CDs in actual sample.
